title: Chestnut and Sausagemeat Stuffing
subtitle: The traditional Christmas stuffing, rich and substantial
excerpt: Chestnut and sausagemeat stuffing is the standard British Christmas stuffing: pork sausagemeat, cooked chestnuts, softened onion, and herbs, baked in a separate dish. It is richer and more substantial than sage and onion stuffing, with the sausagemeat providing fat and flavour and the chestnuts giving an earthy sweetness and texture. It goes with turkey, chicken, and pheasant.
sourceNotes: Traditional British Christmas stuffing. Delia Smith, Complete Cookery Course (1978): chestnut stuffing for turkey. Jane Grigson, English Food (1974): sausagemeat and chestnut stuffing. Classic British roast bird accompaniment.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before baking. Defrost overnight in the fridge and bake from room temperature, adding 10 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble up to 24 hours ahead and refrigerate unbaked. Bake from the fridge, adding 10 minutes to the baking time.

glossaryTerms.definition: Vacuum-packed cooked whole chestnuts are the standard convenient form for stuffing and other savoury dishes. They do not require any preparation beyond crumbling or roughly chopping and have a moist, slightly sweet, earthy flavour.

content.text: The texture of this stuffing depends on how the chestnuts are handled. Left in large pieces they give distinct pockets of sweetness; crumbled into the mix they merge into the sausagemeat and produce a more uniform result. Both are correct; it is a matter of preference.
are reliably good and available through most of the year. Fresh-roasted chestnuts give a slightly better flavour but are seasonal and considerably more work.
Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a saucepan over a low heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook gently for 10 to 12 minutes until soft. Remove from the heat and allow to cool.

Heat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Combine the {{sausagemeat}}, {{chestnut-cooked}}, cooled onion mixture, {{sage}}, thyme leaves, {{breadcrumbs-fresh}}, {{eggs}}, {{sea-salt-flakes}}, and {{black-pepper}} in a large bowl. Mix thoroughly with your hands until evenly combined. Press into a buttered baking dish in an even layer. Bake for 40 to 45 minutes until the top is well browned and the stuffing is cooked through to 75°C at the centre.

rows.cause: High fat content sausagemeat releasing excess fat during baking
rows.fix: Pour off any excess fat at the 30-minute mark and continue baking. Use a good-quality pork sausagemeat with a reasonable fat-to-meat ratio.

rows.cause: Insufficient fat or egg in the mix
rows.fix: The sausagemeat provides most of the binding; if it is very lean, add another beaten egg or a little more butter when mixing.

content.text: Chestnut and sausagemeat stuffing is the stuffing for the British Christmas table, appearing alongside the turkey as reliably as Brussels sprouts. It also works well with a large roast chicken or a brace of pheasants. The chestnut season in Britain runs from October through to December, which is why the stuffing is so strongly associated with the autumn and Christmas period.
